questions.question: Explain the concept of a file in operating systems and list any six file attributes.
keyPoints: File is a named collection of related information stored on secondary storage
File provides logical view of data abstraction
Attributes: Name, Identifier, Type, Location, Size, Protection, Timestamps, Owner

questions.question: Differentiate between sequential access and direct (random) access methods with examples of storage media suited for each.
keyPoints: Sequential: Records accessed in order, must read all preceding records - suited for magnetic tape
Direct: Records accessed directly by address - suited for magnetic disks
Direct access uses formula: address = base + (record number × record size)
Sequential is slower for random access, direct is slower for sequential processing

questions.question: What is a file descriptor? Explain the purpose of open() and close() operations.
keyPoints: File descriptor is a small non-negative integer identifying open file
Open() returns file descriptor, checks permissions, loads FCB, sets position to beginning
Close() flushes buffers, updates metadata, releases file descriptor
File descriptor is process-specific and reused after close

questions.question: Describe the indexed file access method. How does it improve search performance?
keyPoints: Uses an index file containing key values and pointers to data blocks
Two-level access: first search index, then access data block directly
Combines benefits of sequential and direct access
Suitable for large files requiring frequent random lookups

questions.question: What are the three timestamps maintained for a file? Explain when each is updated.
keyPoints: Creation time: Set when file is first created
Modification time (mtime): Updated when file content changes
Access time (atime): Updated when file is read or written
Operating systems maintain these in inode/FCB

questions.question: Explain the purpose of file buffering in operating systems. How does it improve I/O performance?
keyPoints: Buffers temporarily store data in memory before disk write or after disk read
Reduces number of physical I/O operations by grouping small reads/writes
Write-behind: data written to buffer, actual disk write delayed
Read-ahead: anticipated data prefetched into buffer
Improves throughput and reduces disk access overhead

questions.question: What is an inode in Unix/Linux file systems? List any five pieces of information stored in an inode.
keyPoints: Inode (index node) is a data structure storing file metadata
Contains: File type, permissions, owner ID, group ID, file size, timestamps (atime, mtime, ctime), block pointers, link count, extended attributes
Each file has unique inode number
Directory entries map filenames to inode numbers

questions.question: Discuss the various file operations performed by an operating system. Explain how the file control block (FCB) is used in managing these operations.
keyPoints: File operations: Create, Delete, Open, Close, Read, Write, Seek, Rename, Truncate
Create: allocates FCB, initializes attributes, creates directory entry
Open: validates permissions, loads FCB into memory, returns file descriptor
Read/Write: transfer data between memory and file, update position pointer
Seek: reposition file pointer for direct access
Close: flush buffers, update FCB to disk, release descriptor
FCB contains all file metadata and current state - central to all operations

questions.question: Compare hard links and soft (symbolic) links in Unix/Linux file systems. Discuss their advantages, limitations, and behavior when the original file is deleted.
keyPoints: Hard links: Multiple directory entries pointing to same inode, share data blocks, same inode number
Soft links: Special file containing path to target, different inode number, can cross filesystem boundaries
Hard link limitations: Cannot link across filesystems, cannot link to directories (without special privileges)
Soft link limitations: Broken if target deleted/moved, overhead of reading link content
When original deleted: Hard link still accesses data until all links deleted; Soft link becomes broken pointer
Link count in inode tracks number of hard links

questions.question: Explain the three major file access methods: Sequential, Direct/Random, and Indexed. Discuss the advantages, disadvantages, and suitable applications of each.
keyPoints: Sequential: Records accessed one after another, simple implementation, suitable for tape and batch processing, inefficient for random access
Direct: Calculate record address directly, very fast random access, requires fixed-length records, suitable for disks, causes external fragmentation
Indexed: Use index file for pointers, two-step access, combines sequential and direct benefits, index overhead, suitable for large databases with multiple access patterns
Choice depends on: access pattern (sequential vs random), storage medium, record size, file size, frequency of updates

questions.question: What is file locking? Explain different types of locks and discuss the problems of improper file locking in multi-process environments.
keyPoints: File locking prevents concurrent access conflicts in multi-process systems
Shared Lock (Read Lock): Multiple processes can read simultaneously, prevents exclusive write
Exclusive Lock (Write Lock): Only one process can access, prevents both read and write
Mandatory vs Advisory locking: OS enforces mandatory; Advisory requires processes to cooperate
Problems without locking: Race conditions, data corruption, lost updates, inconsistent file state
Example: Two processes writing to same file simultaneously can corrupt data or lose updates
